segments.s499-g33: 'بکشتند' is the third‑person plural past tense 'they killed'. 'فرجام کارش' is a genitival phrase meaning 'the end/outcome of his affair' (literal word order: 'end his‑affair'), and 'به تیر' is an instrumental phrase 'with/by an arrow' where 'به' here has the instrumental force. Together the clause says they ended his affairs by an arrow.
segments.s499-g34: This is a poetic noun phrase describing that arrow. 'یکی' = 'one' / 'a certain' qualifying the following; 'آهنین' = 'iron' modifies it. The remaining words form a string of epithets: 'کوه' (mountain‑like), 'بد' (ill/terrible/strong), 'پیل‌گیر' literally 'elephant‑seizer' — a compound in which 'گیر' works as '‑catcher/seizer'. The phrase therefore paints the arrow as a single iron missile, mountain‑like and capable of seizing elephants.
segments.s499-g35: 'وزان' = 'from that' (spatial/derivational), introducing the place from which the following action happens. 'جایگه' = 'place' (the noun being referenced). 'تیز لشکر' = 'the swift army' (adjective before noun). 'براند' is an older/epic-form verb from راندن used here to mean 'drove/put in motion' or 'set forth' (third person, linking to 'لشکر' as its subject); the prefix 'بـ' is a classical narrative marker often seen in Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s499-g36: 'بسی' is an adverb meaning 'many/much' or 'loudly/much so' in classical Persian (not matched exactly by the provided senses). 'نام دادار گیهان' is a genitival noun phrase, 'the name of the Creator of the world' (نام + [دادارِ گیهان]). 'بخواند' is the narrative third‑person form of خواندن here meaning 'he/they recited/uttered/called' — so the clause means they loudly recited or invoked the Creator's name.
segments.s499-g1: وزان is the poetic contraction (وز + آن) meaning "from that/and from that." The clause uses VSO order typical in older/poetic Persian: رفت (past tense) is the verb ‘went/departed’ placed before its subject خورشیدفش, which here functions as a name or epithet. جایگه is a locative noun 'place' that is governed by وزان.
segments.s499-g2: بیامد is a simple past 'came/arrived.' دمان (literally 'hem, edge') marks the point he reached; تا + noun phrase gives the limit/extent ('as far as'), so تا زمین حبش indicates the destination/extent 'as far as the land of Habash.' Word order is straightforward: verb then place phrases that follow to give destination.
segments.s499-g3: ز before مردم زمین is the poetic genitive/relational marker 'of/among' (like 'از' in classical usage). مردم زمین is a genitive phrase 'the people of the land.' بود is the past copula 'were.' چون introduces a simile 'like' and پر زاغ is the simile's noun phrase 'a feather of the crow' (i.e. 'crow‑feathers').
segments.s499-g4: سیه گشته is a compound of adjective + perfect participle meaning 'have become black/turned black' used predicatively. و simply links the two predicates. چشمها is the subject of the following simile; چون چراغ 'like lamps' is a straightforward simile comparing their eyes to lights.
segments.s499-g5: این عبارت (تناور یکی لشکری زورمند) uses یکی in the indefinite sense 'a certain/one' before a noun phrase; it introduces a single individual from the host. تناور is an adjective 'tall/towering' modifying that one; لشکری here marks association with the army (one of the army/army‑man), and زورمند is another adjective 'powerful, mighty.' Word order is adjective + determiner + noun + adjective (typical in poetic compression).
segments.s499-g6: This is a string of nominal attributes describing the subject: برهنه تن 'naked (in) body' and پوست 'and skin' are parallel nouns/adjectives; و links them. بالابلند is a compound adjective 'high‑winged/lofty' appended to continue the descriptive list. The lack of an explicit verb is a poetic verbless description that attaches to the previously introduced 'one.'
segments.s499-g7: چو here means 'when' (temporal). از دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar'; دیدند is the past plural verb 'they saw.' گرد سپاه: گرد is used as 'dust' (the dust raised by an approaching host) and is directly linked to سپاه 'the army' (i.e. 'the army's dust'), so the clause literally reads 'when they from afar saw the army's dust.'
segments.s499-g8: خروشی is a noun 'a roar/clamor'; برآمد is a past verb 'arose/issued forth.' ز here is the classical/prepositional 'from,' governing ابر سیاه 'a black cloud' (ابَر + adjective سیاه). The clause therefore describes a sound issuing forth from a dark cloud.
segments.s499-g9: سپاه انجمن شد هزاران هزار is a compact, poetic clause: سپاه (subject) + انجمن شد (became/assembled into an assembly). هزاران هزار is a reduplicative expression meaning 'thousands upon thousands' and functions as an emphatic quantifier modifying the assembled host. Note the past tense شد marks a completed change of state.
segments.s499-g10: وران here is a connective/temporal particle (poetic) meaning roughly 'then/again' linking the next clause; تیره شد is a compound with تیره (dark/ominous) + شد (became) meaning 'became dark/gloomy'; دیده شهریار is the possessed noun phrase 'the king's eye(s)' with the possessed noun (دیده) before the possessor (شهریار), a common Persian order. The whole clause reads 'then the king's eyes became dark.'
segments.s499-g11: به سوی سکندر نهادند سر is literal 'to/toward Alexander they set (their) head(s)'; به سوی is the prepositional phrase 'toward', نهادند is the past tense plural of نهادن 'to place/set' used here idiomatically for 'directed/turned', and سر ('head') is an object used metonymically for 'their forces/attention'. Poetic inversion places the prepositional phrase first.
segments.s499-g12: بکشتند is the simple past 'they killed' (verb before object by poetic order). بسیار پرخاشخر is the object: بسیار (many) + پرخاشخر (quarrelsome ones/violent men); note that in poetry the quantifier can follow the verb and govern the following noun phrase.
segments.s499-g13: به جای سنان استخوان داشتند is a prepositional phrase 'به جای' meaning 'in place of / instead of' + سنان (spear/spearhead) followed by استخوان داشتند 'they had bones'. داشتند is the past-tense plural 'they had/possessed'. The phrase means 'they had bones instead of spears.'
segments.s499-g14: همی is a classical/prosodic particle marking ongoing or habitual action (here simple narrative flavor); بر تن مرد بگذاشتند is 'they placed upon the bodies of men' with the preposition بر + تن (body) + مرد (man) and the past verb بگذاشتند 'they put/laid'. Word order is object before verb, typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s499-g15: به لشکر بفرمود پس شهریار is arranged with the indirect object phrase (به لشکر 'to the army') + the honoriﬁc past verb بفرمود 'he said/commanded', then پس شهریار 'then the king' (poetic inversion placing the speaker/subject after the verb). بفرمود is an elevated register for 'he ordered/said.'
segments.s499-g16: که introduces a reported-content clause 'that'; برداشتند is past plural 'they took up/removed' (here 'took up' = assumed their weapons); آلت کارزار is a genitive phrase 'instruments of battle' (آلت = instrument/weapon, کارزار = battle/struggle). Together: 'that they take up the instruments of battle.'
segments.s499-g17: This clause is a simple verbal clause with the subject (حبش, ‘the Abyssinian’) placed at the end for poetic emphasis. برهنه is an adjectival predicate placed before the motion verb آمد and qualifying the subject (‘naked’). به + جنگ + اندر آمد is a standard classical sequence meaning ‘entered into the battle’ (به…اندر آمد = ‘came into’). Note the archaic particle اندر used with به to mark motion into/within.
segments.s499-g18: This short result clause uses the light verb گشت (become) after the noun غمی to mean ‘became sorrow’ or ‘was filled with grief’. زان = ‘from that’ links this clause back to the previous event. لشکر is the possessor/agent phrase that follows زان (زان لشکر … = ‘for that army …’); شیرفش is an epithet or name attached to لشکر and here functions as its identifier.
segments.s499-g19: بکشتند is a perfective past-tense verb ‘they killed’. زیشان (ز + ایشان fused) means ‘of them / from them’ and functions as the object-marking phrase. فزون از شمار is an idiomatic quantitative phrase: فزون ‘excessive/more’ + از شمار ‘than the count/than can be numbered’ — together ‘more than could be counted’. Word order places the verb first (typical in epic lines) followed by its object and the quantifier.
segments.s499-g20: This clause uses the intransitive/pseudo-reflexive بپیچید (past ‘turned’) with سر ‘head’ as the thing that turns: بپیچید سر از کارزار = ‘(their) heads turned away from the battle’. دیگر here means ‘other’ or ‘many others’ (an indefinite pluralizing sense). از marks source or separation ‘from the battlefield’. The sequence expresses withdrawal/aversion from combat.
segments.s499-g21: This causal clause begins with the poetic preposition-like ز (= از) ‘from/because of’ plus the verbal noun ریختن ‘the spilling/shedding’ to form ‘from the shedding of blood’. گشت is the resultative/copular ‘became’. روی زمین is the nominal phrase ‘the face/surface of the earth’ with روی as noun ‘surface’ followed by زمین.
segments.s499-g22: سراسر ‘throughout/entirely’ modifies the following simile. به کردار = ‘in the manner of / like’ (به used adverbially here). دریای چین is a metaphor: دریای ‘sea of’ + چین ‘wrinkles/folds’, so ‘like a sea of wrinkles’ (a poetic image for a rippled, blood-soaked ground).
segments.s499-g23: چو ‘when’ introduces a temporal subordinate clause. از خون marks the cause/means ‘with/from blood’. در و دشت is a fixed classical pairing meaning roughly ‘town (or enclosure) and plain’; در here is the lexical item ‘door/gate’ used elliptically to mean inhabited place, paired with دشت ‘plain’. آلوده شد is the passive/resultative compound ‘became stained/defiled’.
segments.s499-g24: This main clause begins with ز (poetic for از) ‘from/of’ + کشته used as a substantive ‘the slain’. به هر جای = ‘in/to every place’ (به marking location/extension). بر توده شد uses بر + توده ‘upon a heap’ with شد ‘became/was made’ meaning ‘were heaped up’ — i.e. ‘heaps of the slain formed everywhere’. The order places the circumstantial locution before the resultative verb phrase.
segments.s499-g25: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. The phrase بر توده خاشاکها is a locative/goal phrase ('upon/at the heaps of chaff') and برزدند is a 3rd-person plural past verb 'they struck/attacked' (بر‑ as a prefix + زدند). Word order is conjunction + locative phrase + verb (typical classical Persian).
segments.s499-g26: بفرمود is an honorific/past reporting verb 'he ordered/said'. تا here = 'so that / in order that' introducing the purpose/result clause. آتش اندر زدند uses اندر 'in/within' plus زدند; together this is the idiom corresponding to 'they set fire (into it)' (classical collocation like آتش زدن). The verb زدند is past plural and in this context means 'kindled/laid fire to'.
segments.s499-g27: چو شب گشت = 'when night became/arrived' (گشت past of 'become'). بشنید آواز گرگ is an impersonal/past reporting clause 'the voice/howl of the wolf was heard' (بشنید = 'heard' / 'it was heard'); the order places the verb before its object phrase (poetic inversion).
segments.s499-g28: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. بپوشید is past 'he covered/put on' (here likely 'wrapped himself' or 'covered himself'). خفتان and ترگ are the things he covered/put on: خفتان functions as a sleeping-cover/blanket in context (an older usage), and ترگ is 'a rag'. The conjunction و links the two objects. Note: خفتان is archaic/poetic as a noun here.
segments.s499-g29: یکی پیش رو بود = 'one (a certain man) was in front/foremost' (یکی = 'a certain one'; پیش رو = 'in front/foremost'). مهتر ز پیل is a nominal predicate 'a chief/leader greater than an elephant' where ز is the poetic preposition 'than/from' used for comparison.
segments.s499-g30: به سر is the locative 'on (his) head'. بر سرو داشت is a fused predicate: بر سرو (literally 'like a cypress' / 'cypress-wise') + داشت 'he had' — i.e. he bore on his head something tall/erect like a cypress. همرنگ نیل is an adjectival phrase 'the same color as indigo' (همرنگ = 'same-colored', نیل = 'indigo').
segments.s499-g31: ازین نامداران = 'from these famed/renowned men' (ازین = 'of/from these', نامداران = 'the famous ones'). فراوان here qualifies the quantity 'many/abundant'. بکشت is an archaic past form meaning 'he slew/killed' (classical past of کشتن with the prefixed ب‑).
segments.s499-g32: بسی حمله بردند = 'they carried out many attacks' (بسی = 'many' (classical), حمله = 'attack', بردند = past plural 'they carried/undertook' used here for 'launched'). و links the clauses. ننمود پشت literally 'did not show back' = 'did not turn their backs / did not retreat'; ننمود is negative past of 'show', پشت = 'back/behind' used idiomatically for 'retreat'.

sentences.s499-s17: They killed him — the end of his affairs — with an arrow: a single iron one, mountain‑sized, fit to seize elephants.
sentences.s499-s18: From that place the swift army set forth, and loudly invoked the name of the Creator of the world.
sentences.s499-s1: From that place Khurshidfash departed; he came to the hem/foothill as far as the land of Habash (Abyssinia).
sentences.s499-s2: The people of the land were black like crow‑feathers, and their eyes were like lamps.
sentences.s499-s3: A tall, mighty fighter of the host—naked in body and skin and lofty‑winged.
sentences.s499-s4: When from afar they saw the army's dust, a roar rose from the black cloud.
sentences.s499-s5: The army assembled into a mass, thousands upon thousands; then the king's eyes grew dark.
sentences.s499-s6: They turned toward Alexander and killed many quarrelsome men.
sentences.s499-s7: They had bones in place of spearheads, and they laid them upon the bodies of men.
sentences.s499-s8: Then the king commanded the army that they should take up the instruments of battle.
sentences.s499-s9: The Abyssinian came into the fight naked; from that the army of Shirafsh was filled with grief.
sentences.s499-s10: They killed of them more than could be counted; other heads turned away from the battlefield.
sentences.s499-s11: From the spilling of blood the surface of the earth became everywhere like a sea of wrinkles.
sentences.s499-s12: When town and plain were stained with blood, heaps of the slain rose up in every place.
sentences.s499-s13: When they struck the piles of chaff, he ordered that they set fire to them.
sentences.s499-s14: When night fell, the howl of the wolf was heard; Alexander wrapped himself (for sleep) in a covering and a rag.
sentences.s499-s15: One in front was a chief greater than an elephant; on his head he bore (a plume) like a cypress, the color of indigo.
sentences.s499-s16: From these renowned men he slew many; they made numerous assaults and did not show (their) back (i.e. they did not turn their backs).
